output_text:
Cleaned up the section: tightened run-on sentences, removed hedge-stacking ("somewhat," "arguably," "presumably," "we believe that"), and replaced vague claims with concrete phrasing. Flagged places where exact numbers should be filled in.

---

We designed a human evaluation protocol to assess the quality of our model's open-ended story continuations, since automatic metrics are unreliable for this task. Crowdworkers recruited from [PLATFORM] rated continuations on coherence and fluency using a five-point scale. Each worker saw a story prefix alongside three candidate continuations drawn from different systems.

We sampled 480 prefixes from the held-out set, collecting roughly three judgments per prefix. Inter-annotator agreement was [VALUE] ([METRIC, e.g., Krippendorff's α]), which we consider acceptable for a task of this difficulty. A more detailed agreement analysis is left to future work.

Our system receives higher coherence scores than both baselines ([Δ = X.X, p < 0.0Y]), while fluency scores are comparable across systems. We read this as evidence that the proposed decoding modification improves coherence without sacrificing fluency.

The pattern holds across prefix-length buckets (see Table [N]), indicating that the advantage is not driven by a particular input regime.

---

Uncertainty disclosure: I introduced placeholder fields ([PLATFORM], the exact agreement value/metric, effect size, p-value, table number) because the original text was vague about them; verify these against your actual experimental results. I also inferred that "480" and "three workers per prefix" are exact rather than approximate — confirm before publication. The interpretation of fluency parity as a positive finding mirrors the original's framing; if a significance test shows fluency is meaningfully lower for your system, that claim needs revision.
